Q1: I tried to keep a pretty structured routine, honestly. I'd set aside about three hours a day for studying, usually in the mornings when my mind felt clearest. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making color-coded notes and diagrams. But even with all that planning, I often felt like I wasn't doing enough, which made me anxious and sometimes threw off my whole schedule.
Q2: I really tried to stick to regular times, but my pattern definitely shifted when deadlines approached. Early in the semester I was more consistent, studying the same blocks each day. But as assignments piled up, I'd find myself cramming at odd hours, which stressed me out even more. The anxiety of not knowing if I was doing enough made it hard to maintain that steady rhythm I wanted.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I was worried I'd missed something important—an announcement, a deadline, or feedback on my work. That fear of falling behind was always in the back of my mind. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if grades were posted, even when I knew it was too early. The LMS became this thing I checked compulsively when I was feeling scared about my performance.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS kind of became automatic for me, especially first thing in the morning and before bed. It started because I was anxious about missing deadlines, but then it just became habit. I'd open it on my phone without even thinking about it. I'm not sure if that's healthy, but it felt like the only way to calm my nerves about staying on top of things.
Q5: The video lectures were really helpful for me since I learn best visually. I watched over 200 minutes of them, often pausing to take screenshots for my notes. The discussion forums were also useful because seeing how other students interpreted things helped me feel less alone in my confusion. When peers explained concepts differently than the professor, sometimes it just clicked better for me.
Q6: I tended to skip over the reading-heavy PDFs and text-based resources. They just didn't stick in my brain the way videos and diagrams did. I'd download them with good intentions but then never really engage deeply with them. I know I should have used them more, but when you're already anxious about time, you gravitate toward what feels most efficient for your learning style.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to an upcoming assignment or exam. Visual materials, practice problems with feedback, and anything that helped me create study aids—those felt valuable. If something seemed abstract or disconnected from what I'd be assessed on, I struggled to justify spending time on it when I was already stressed about everything else.
Q8: Required activities felt like they had to be done no matter what, so I prioritized them even when I was exhausted or anxious. Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford—like, I wanted to explore them, but the fear of not completing mandatory work always won out. There was this constant guilt about not doing the optional stuff, which honestly just added to my anxiety rather than making me feel free to explore.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then study from them on my tablet without being connected to the LMS. I also made extensive handwritten notes and flashcards that I'd review constantly. My room is covered in sticky notes with diagrams and key concepts. A lot of my actual learning happened with these physical materials spread out around me, not while I was logged in anywhere.
Q10: When I was reviewing my handwritten notes or redrawing diagrams to memorize processes, none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd spend hours going through flashcards or teaching concepts to myself out loud. Sometimes I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then replay my own screen recording of it multiple times offline. The system only saw me watch it once, but I probably reviewed it five or six times.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save a video, watch or read it once while logged in, and then that material would become part of my offline study rotation for weeks. I preferred studying without internet distractions anyway—it helped with my anxiety to not see notifications popping up. So one download might represent hours and hours of actual engagement that happened completely offline.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. My anxiety spiked, and I started feeling scared that no matter how much I studied, it wouldn't be enough. I'd sit down to work and just freeze, overwhelmed by everything I needed to do. My resilience isn't great, so when things piled up, I kind of shut down instead of pushing through. Those were the weeks I felt most disconnected from the course.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. The workload from my two online courses sometimes collided, and I'd have to make impossible choices about what to prioritize. My stress levels made it hard to focus, so even when I was technically using the LMS, my engagement was pretty shallow during those periods.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered to all the other things stressing me out. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing what anyone was saying. It was like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something, but I wasn't actually learning. The anxiety made it hard to be mentally present even when I was physically there.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even though the guilt ate at me. I think it was fear of not doing them well enough—if I didn't start, I couldn't fail yet. My assignment completion rate wasn't great because of this. I'd know something was important, feel terrified of messing it up, and then avoid it until the deadline forced my hand. It's a terrible cycle that my anxiety creates.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could override my avoidance. I'd have intense bursts of LMS activity in the days before something was due. My login frequency probably spiked dramatically around those times. It wasn't healthy—I'd be scared and stressed, rushing through things—but deadlines gave me the external pressure I needed to actually submit work instead of just worrying about it.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. When it was positive, I felt relieved and more motivated to engage. But when it was critical, even constructively so, I'd spiral into anxiety and sometimes avoid logging in for a day or two because I was scared to see more. The peer support intervention helped a bit—having classmates to process feedback with made it less terrifying to face.
Q18: The course structure was pretty clear, which I appreciated because ambiguity makes my anxiety worse. Having a predictable schedule of when materials were released helped me plan. But the structure also meant there was always something due, which kept my stress levels constantly elevated. I never felt like I could fully relax because there was always the next thing looming.
Q19: The visual resources and video content made engagement easier for someone like me. The discussion forums also helped because I could connect with peers, which was important since the peer support intervention was part of my experience. What made it harder was the sheer volume of materials—sometimes I felt paralyzed by how much there was to get through, even when each individual piece was well-designed.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my anxious days—logging in repeatedly to check things, watching videos, participating in discussions, but then being too scared or overwhelmed to actually submit the assignment. The fear of not doing it perfectly can be paralyzing. You engage with everything around the task but avoid the task itself because completion means being judged.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. If someone learns effectively offline, their LMS activity would be low but their understanding could be solid. I performed reasonably well on exams even though my assignment completion was inconsistent, partly because I did so much studying outside the system. The LMS doesn't capture someone reviewing notes at 2 AM or teaching themselves from downloaded materials.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't tell if you're anxiously refreshing the page versus deeply focused on learning. They definitely can't capture the emotional experience of being a student—the fear, the stress, the moments of genuine understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious and want to learn more, not just scared of failing. Real engagement for me is when I'm making connections between concepts, creating study materials because I want to remember things, and feeling some sense of investment in my own learning beyond just the grade.
Q24: The LMS can't show how scared I am most of the time, or how much my anxiety shapes every interaction I have with the course. It can't capture the hours I spend studying offline, the peer support conversations that helped me feel less alone, or the mental energy I expend just trying to manage my stress enough to function. My engagement is so much more than my digital footprint suggests.
